data.title: Swiss watch industry sees tough times ahead over Trump tariffs
data.description:
Key points:
  • Switzerland slapped with 31% tariffs by Trump
  • Entry-mid level Swiss watches could be most impacted
  • Swiss Watch industry nervous as U.S. is biggest export market

Swiss watch seller Sacha Davidoff was scrambling on Thursday to understand what new U.S. tariffs on Switzerland announced by President Donald Trump meant for the goods he was due to export from his shop in Geneva this week.

"I made a deal and now we're stuck. We are supposed to ship the watch, but what do we do now? He (the client) won't want to pay 31% more. It doesn't feel real," Davidoff said.

Shares in European luxury companies dropped on Thursday after Trump announced import tariffs, including 31% on goods from Switzerland and 20% from the European Union.

The vintage watches Davidoff sells are worth an average of 20,000 Swiss francs ($23,272) and could now be subject to another 6,000 Swiss francs in import tax.

JPMorgan analysts said they expected to see most pressure on the Swiss watchmakers, with both Cartier-owner Richemont and Swatch Group already on thin margins. Richemont did not reply to a request for comment. Swatch did not comment.

The U.S. is Switzerland's top foreign watch market, accounting for 16.8% of exports, or 4.4 billion francs worth, according to Federation of the Swiss Watch Industry.

Davidoff said he sells on average about 250 vintage watches by Rolex, Piaget and Patek Philippe annually and that the U.S. accounts for 60% of his business.

"I'm assuming that retailers are freaked out over there too," said Davidoff. "It is just going to put the market into complete freeze, or free fall."

TENSION

At a large conference hall in Geneva on Thursday where thousands of people flocked to admire the latest products being promoted by Swiss brands like Rolex, Cartier and Chopard, industry insiders were fretting about the future.

"It's been tense. Some meetings with Americans have been cancelled. Sales will surely be down. The year ahead doesn't look very promising for the watch industry," said Clement Fehrenbacher of Le Cercle des Horlogers.

The Neuchatel-based company manufactures the small parts inside watches for big watch brands in Switzerland.

"It'll be very difficult to get new projects if the market doesn't recover," Fehrenbacher said. "One of our clients is American, and we're waiting to see."

Behind the steady flow of champagne and glittering watches at the conference, concern was palpable among watchmakers after an already challenging 2024, which saw sales to China, the no. 2 market, decline by more than a quarter.

Many of the big names setting out their stalls in Geneva opted to remain silent about Trump's trade measures.

Vacheron Constantin marked its 270th birthday at the Watches and Wonders show by presenting an intricate new wristwatch boasting more than 1,521 component parts. But it declined to comment on the tariffs, as did those of other famous names.

"The U.S. market is absolutely critical," said David Sadigh, founder and CEO of Digital Luxury Group, a business consultancy. "With potential slowdown domestically in China, I think that you can feel some nervousness."

($1 = 0.8566 Swiss francs)

data.title: Author pledges portion of book royalties to Thurgood Marshall College Fund; presidents and chancellors presented with copies at HBCU Fly-In
data.description:
Washington, D.C., April 03, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — The author of an authoritative history of historically Black colleges and universities (HBCUs) pledged a portion of the royalties from her book to the Thurgood Marshall College Fund (TMCF). Inspired by the work and the gesture, Dr. Harry L. Williams, president & CEO of TMCF, presented copies of the book to presidents and chancellors of two- and four-year HBCUs and historically Black community colleges (HBCCs). 

Deondra Rose, author of “The Power of Black Excellence: HBCUs and the Fight for American Democracy,” pledged 50 percent of the royalties of the book to support TMCF. Rose is the Kevin D. Gorter Associate Professor of Public Policy at the Duke University Sanford School of Public Policy.  

Rose’s book looks at the history of HBCUs and the role they’ve played in enhancing American democracy. It was released in September 2024 and received a review in Kirkus Reviews.

The author connects HBCUs and the development of American democracy in several ways, including empowering Black citizens; reshaping political power; fostering political leadership; and challenging conventional wisdom. Rose also stresses that policy makers promote democracy itself by supporting HBCUs.  

The book was a prominent topic of TMCF’s annual Presidents and Chancellors HBCU Fly-In in Washington, D.C., as Dr. Williams presented each attending president and chancellor with a copy. 

“We’re honored that Dr. Rose included TMCF in this way in her illuminating look at the long-term effect HBCUs have on Black life and success,” Dr. Williams said. “It’s important and necessary to spread her message to our colleagues at our member schools, from the administration down to the newest students.” 

“The Power of Black Excellence” is also the inaugural recipient of the Dr. N. Joyce Payne Research Center (Payne Center) Book of the Quarter award. The Payne Center’s Book of the Quarter award recognizes English-language books published within the past two years that relate to equity and justice, and/or are grounded in scholarship from or about HBCUs.

“Dr. Rose’s book provides a critical historical context that complements the quantitative data and policy insights generated by our research,” Dr. M.C. Brown II, executive director and research scientist at the Payne Center, said. “It underscores the vital role HBCUs play in shaping American democracy and fostering economic opportunity. Her work is a significant contribution to understanding the multifaceted impact of these institutions.”

“The Power of Black Excellence” is available from Oxford University Press, Amazon.com and other major booksellers.

About Thurgood Marshall College Fund

Established in 1987, Thurgood Marshall College Fund (TMCF) is the nation’s largest organization exclusively representing the Black college community. TMCF member schools include the publicly supported historically Black colleges and universities, predominantly Black institutions and historically Black community colleges, enrolling nearly 80% of all students attending Black colleges and universities. Through scholarships, capacity building and research initiatives, innovative programs and strategic partnerships, TMCF is a vital resource in the K-12 and higher education space. The organization is also the source of top employers seeking top talent for competitive internships and good jobs. TMCF is a 501(c)(3) tax-exempt, charitable organization. For more information about TMCF, visit www.tmcf.org.

Clara Ross Stamps

The Thurgood Marshall College Fund

data.title: Auto shares slide as first set of tariffs kick in
data.description:
** Shares of auto companies slide as U.S. President Donald Trump's previously announced 25% tariffs on auto imports activate

** U.S.-based shares of Stellantis down 7.5%, Ford falls 3.8%, General Motors down 2.5%

** Tariffs could cost consumers more than $30 billion in higher vehicle prices and reduce car sales in the first full year, consultancy firm Anderson Economic Group said

** Tariffs on auto parts, which activate on May 3, will cover more than $460 billion worth of imports of vehicles and auto parts imports annually

** Reciprocal tariffs announced by Trump do not stack atop the auto tariffs, said James Picariello, analyst at BNP Paribas Exane

** Added that the current tariffs would affect 50% of all vehicles sold in the U.S. and raise average price of a new vehicle by about 7% or $3200

NEW YORK, April 03, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) —

WHY: Rosen Law Firm, a global investor rights law firm, announces the filing of a class action lawsuit on behalf of purchasers of securities of Actinium Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (NYSE American: ATNM) between October 31, 2022, and August 2, 2024, both dates inclusive (the “Class Period”). A class action lawsuit has already been filed. If you wish to serve as lead plaintiff, you must move the Court no later than May 26, 2025.

SO WHAT: If you purchased Actinium securities during the Class Period you may be entitled to compensation without payment of any out of pocket fees or costs through a contingency fee arrangement.

WHAT TO DO NEXT: To join the Actinium class action, go to https://rosenlegal.com/submit-form/?case_id=37511 or call Phillip Kim, Esq. toll-free at 866-767-3653 or email case@rosenlegal.com for information on the class action. A class action lawsuit has already been filed. If you wish to serve as lead plaintiff, you must move the Court no later than May 26, 2025. A lead plaintiff is a representative party acting on behalf of other class members in directing the litigation.

WHY ROSEN LAW: We encourage investors to select qualified counsel with a track record of success in leadership roles. Often, firms issuing notices do not have comparable experience, resources, or any meaningful peer recognition. Be wise in selecting counsel. The Rosen Law Firm represents investors throughout the globe, concentrating its practice in securities class actions and shareholder derivative litigation. Rosen Law Firm achieved the largest ever securities class action settlement against a Chinese Company at the time. Rosen Law Firm was Ranked No. 1 by ISS Securities Class Action Services for number of securities class action settlements in 2017. The firm has been ranked in the top 4 each year since 2013 and has recovered hundreds of millions of dollars for investors. In 2019 alone the firm secured over $438 million for investors. In 2020, founding partner Laurence Rosen was named by law360 as a Titan of Plaintiffs’ Bar. Many of the firm’s attorneys have been recognized by Lawdragon and Super Lawyers.

DETAILS OF THE CASE: According to the lawsuit, during the Class Period, defendants made false and/or misleading statements and/or failed to disclose that: (1) Actinium’s data from Sierra Trial was unlikely to satisfy the U.S. Food and Drug Administration’s (“FDA”) guidelines for the acceptance and approval of Actinium’s Iomab-B Biologics License Application (“BLA”); (2) the additional analyses, including long-term follow-ups that purportedly demonstrated a trend towards improved Overall Survival (“OS”) that Actinium provided to the FDA in an attempt to mitigate the Sierra Trial’s poor OS data were unlikely to satisfy the FDA’s guidelines for the acceptance and approval of Actinium’s Iomab-B BLA; (3) as a result, the FDA would likely refuse to review the Iomab-B BLA or, if it did consider that BLA, that the application in its current form was unlikely to be approved; and (4) as a result, defendants’ positive statements about Actinium’s business, operations, and prospects were materially misleading and/or lacked a reasonable basis. data.title: OPEC+ to Make Larger Output Hike in May, Oil TumblesCommodities Roundup
data.description:
MARKET MOVEMENTS:

  • Brent crude oil falls 6.9% to $69.79 a barrel.
  • European benchmark gas is down 4.7% to 39.34 euros a megawatt hour.
  • Gold futures are down 1.1% to $3,131.60 a troy ounce.
  • LME three-month copper futures are down 3.4% to $9,366 a metric ton.

TOP STORY:

OPEC+ to Make Larger Output Hike in May, Oil Tumbles

The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries and its allies agreed to make a larger oil output hike in May in an unexpected move that further dragged on tariff-hit crude prices.

After a meeting held online on Thursday, eight OPEC+ countries said they will increase output by 411,000 barrels a day next month--the equivalent of three monthly increments--citing "healthy market fundamentals" and a "positive market outlook."

In afternoon trade in Europe, Brent crude slumped 7.1% to $69.63 a barrel, while West Texas Intermediate was down 7.8% to $66.15 a barrel. Losses are also fueled by widespread fears that U.S. President Trump's latest tariff blitz could dampen global economic growth and demand for oil.

OTHER STORIES:

AIP Management Pledges $500 Million to Solar Developer Silicon Ranch

European infrastructure investor AIP Management has committed about $500 million to solar developer Silicon Ranch, betting that increased demand for electricity in the U.S. will continue to lift the country's renewable-energy industry.

Nashville, Tenn.-based Silicon Ranch operates solar and battery projects across the U.S. and Canada, particularly in the U.S. Southern states of Tennessee, Georgia, Mississippi, Arkansas and Kentucky. Existing installations have a total power capacity of 3.6 gigawatts, slightly more than a third of what New York City takes during the summer, and a similar amount of capacity is in the works through the company's pipeline of projects, according to Silicon Ranch.

--

Gold Retreats But Holds Near Record Highs After Trump's Tariffs

Gold prices pulled back in early trade but remained near record highs as investors seek refuge in the safe-haven asset following U.S. President Trump's latest tariff blitz.

Futures slipped 0.6% to $3,148.10 a troy ounce, likely due to some profit-taking after hitting a fresh peak of $3,196.60 earlier in the trading session.

--

Iraq Says Oil Exports Aren't Covered by Trump Tariffs, Impact Limited

Iraqi oil exports to the U.S. aren't covered by tariffs of 39% imposed by the U.S. on the country. The economic impact of the new tariffs will be "very limited" on the Middle East nation, Iraqi diplomatic adviser Farhad Alaa Al-Din told the state-run Iraqi News Agency.

Iraq has an annual trade surplus of $5.7 billion with the U.S., after its oil sales to the American market doubled in the past two years, Iraqi government adviser Mazhar Mohammed Salih told INA last month.

--

Oil Slumps After Trump's Tariff Blitz

Oil prices fell sharply after U.S. President Trump unveiled a sweeping new tariff plan, stoking fears that escalating trade tensions will hurt global economic growth and demand for crude.

In midday trade in Europe, Brent slumped 4.1% to $71.87 a barrel, while West Texas Intermediate dropped 4.3% to $68.94 a barrel. Shares in major European oil companies followed through, with BP and Shell down 3.2% and 2.6%, respectively. TotalEnergies fell 1.8%, while Eni was down 2%.

--

BP Closes Low-Carbon Mobility Business Development Team

BP will close its low-carbon mobility team as it implements its new strategy.

The British energy company said in an email to staff that it will close the nine-person business development team, which had been examining hydrogen and liquefied natural gas fuel solutions for the transportation sector.

--

Imported Steel and Aluminum Spared From More Tariffs

Imported steel and aluminum already subject to a 25% tariff will be excluded from the new tariffs President Trump announced Wednesday.

Trump last month increased and expanded the tariffs on imported steel and aluminum that he began in 2018. The 25% tariff now covers hundreds of finished products made with imported steel and aluminum.

--

China Says It Has Agreed to EV Tariffs Negotiations With EU

China's commerce ministry has said that China and the European Union have agreed to restart negotiations on electric-vehicle tariffs, coming hot on the heels of Trump's announcement of more tariffs.

The Chinese Ministry of Commerce said at a press conference on Thursday that talks will start as soon as possible, and aim to foster a good environment for Chinese and European companies to invest and work together.

--

U.S. Cattlemen Cheer Trump's Tariff Plan

Some of America's ranchers welcomed President Trump's tariff plan, hoping it will curb beef imports and raise the value of their cattle.

Trump added 10% duties on goods from three of the country's largest beef-trading partners-Brazil, Australia and New Zealand. Australia sent about $3 billion worth of beef to the U.S. last year, nearly double the amount from five years ago, according to the Agriculture Department. America's cattle herds have shrunk in recent years, making livestock more expensive and raising the price of steak and ground beef for consumers.

--

Hershey Nears $750 Million Deal for LesserEvil Popcorn Brand

Hershey is nearing a roughly $750 million deal for the popcorn brand LesserEvil, according to people familiar with the matter.

A deal could be announced soon, granted the talks don't hit any last-minute snags, the people said.

LesserEvil had recently tapped bankers to pursue a sale of its business, which makes a range of snacks using what it says are better-for-you ingredients.

MARKET TALKS:

Cotton Slammed By New Chinese Tariffs — Market Talk

1129 ET - Cotton futures drop 4.4% to 64.8 cents a pound, close to a multi-year low of 63 cents a pound reached early last month. Cotton is more exposed to issues concerning Chinese trade than other agricultural goods, as most U.S. cotton is exported for use in mills overseas. According to USDA data, only 1.7 million bales of cotton were expected to be used domestically for the 2024/25 marketing year, versus 11 million bales to the export market. Canada and Mexico were spared new reciprocal tariffs, but agriculture elsewhere was not. "Agricultural commodities were notable in not receiving a carve-out," says Kimberley Sperrfechter of Capital Economics in a note. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

--

Effect from China Tariffs Not Immediate for Soybeans — Market Talk

1034 ET - The additional 34% tariff levied on Chinese goods is expected to cut into demand for American soybeans, but the timing of President Trump's announcement means that the impact may not be immediate. "China is out of the market for U.S. beans right now anyway," says Matt Zeller of StoneX in a note, saying that China at this time of year is more interested in procuring Brazilian soybeans, as Brazil's harvest of its soybean crop nears its end. But once U.S. farmers plant their soybeans this spring, they may see diminished interest from China--but not a complete absence of demand. "They will need to come back at some point for some of the 2025 crop," says Zeller. CBOT soybeans are down 1.5%.(kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

--

Cocoa Soars, Coffee and Sugar Slide After U.S. Tariffs — Market Talk

1430 GMT - Cocoa and coffee prices are mixed in afternoon trade after U.S. President Trump rattled global markets with harsher-than-expected tariffs on major trading partners, including key commodity producers. Vietnam, Indonesia and Brazil--all major growers of robusta coffee--were targeted with tariffs of 46%, 32% and 10%, respectively. Top cocoa growers Ivory Coast and Ghana face 21% and 10% levies. "Many commodities trade flows from the most affected producers will now enter a labyrinth in order to find demand in other countries at an efficiency cost," says Rabobank's Carlos Mera. "Coffee and chocolate will become more expensive in the U.S." New York-traded cocoa futures soar 4.3% to $9,357 a ton, likely boosted by U.S. dollar weakness, while coffee is down 2.2% to $3.80 a pound. Sugar prices fall 2.5% to 19 cents a pound. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

Oil's Drop Could Bring Challenges for Gulf Economies — Market Talk

1405 GMT - Oil's 6% slump following U.S. President Trump's latest barrage of tariffs signals potential economic challenges for Gulf countries, according to Capital Economics. Jordan, Tunisia and Algeria are the only ones in the region facing reciprocal tariffs in addition to the 10% blanket levy. But beyond their direct impact, tariffs could also hurt demand for oil, a major source of revenue for Gulf nations. In afternoon trade, Brent crude trades 6.1% lower at $70.35 a barrel, while WTI is down 6.6% at $66.99 a barrel. "The downside risks to global oil demand, reflected in a sharp drop in oil prices today, may worry oil producers in the Gulf as they embark on raising oil output," says James Swanston, senior Middle East and North Africa economist. "Saudi Arabia in particular will be watchful of balance of payments pressures after the current account flipped into deficit in 2024." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

U.S. Natural Gas Holds Gains After Storage Build — Market Talk

1115 ET - U.S. natural gas inventories rose for a third consecutive week, narrowing the storage deficit as the winter withdrawal season drew to a close. The EIA reports a 29 Bcf injection into storage for the week ended March 28 to 1,773 Bcf. Stocks were 80 Bcf or 4.3% below the five-year average for the week, and 491 Bcf or 21.7% below their year-earlier level. Analysts in a Wall Street Journal survey had forecast a 25 Bcf injection. Natural gas futures are holding gains while oil and product prices plunge in response to the U.S. tariff blitz. The Nymex front month is up 2.5% at $4.157/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

--

OPEC+ Plan for Bigger May Unwind Adds to Oil's Woes — Market Talk 0933 ET - OPEC and allies including Russia say they plan to speed up the return of withheld barrels in May, increasing output by 411,000 barrels a day, "equivalent to three monthly increments." The decision adds to losses in crude futures already reeling from the U.S. tariff rollout. "The actual increase over prior expectations may weigh on prices near-term but should be seen as a bullish demand indicator," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. The adjustment "signals confidence in the strength of Asian demand, even as macroeconomic sentiment turns negative." WTI is down 7.1% at $66.59 a barrel, and Brent is off 6.6% at $69.97 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

U.S. Natural Gas Looks Immune to Tariff Fallout, for Now — Market Talk

0859 ET - U.S. natural gas futures buck the sinking trend in energy markets as U.S. tariffs and their potential effect on global growth hit oil and products. "While natural gas is partially insulated in the immediate term, the larger consideration within the next 12 months is if macroeconomic disturbance ... will weigh on near-term industrial and power sector demand to the extent that it derails a bullish structural setup for natural gas into winter 2025-26," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. The biggest question, he adds, is how long the tariff framework stays in place, as they give President Trump "massive leverage" in trade negotiations. Nymex natural gas is up 2.3% at $4.149/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

Oil Prices Supported at $70 to $80 a Barrel Range — Market Talk

1234 GMT - Oil prices are likely to remain between $70 to $80 a barrel, Barclay's analysts Lydia Rainforth and Mick Pickup write in a note on the impact of new U.S. tariffs. Oil and gas imports are exempt from the new tariffs but recession fears have hit growth expectations, they write. In light of the potential for lower demand growth but still tighter supply, Brent prices should spend more time above $70 a barrel than below it, they write. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

Oil Futures Sink As U.S. Tariffs Seen Hitting Demand — Market Talk

0825 ET - Oil futures are wiping out the gains of recent weeks as the U.S. "reciprocal tariff" rollout is seen worse than many had expected, raising concerns it will slow the global economy and oil demand with it. "The U.S. consumer is in a bad spot here and overseas manufacturers are in the same boat, which is bearish for demand in an environment that was already struggling," Scott Shelton of TP ICAP says in a note. WTI is down 6.1% at $67.34 a barrel, and Brent is off 5.7%at $70.67 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

Rio Tinto CEO Says Investors Shouldn't Stress About Impact of Tariff War — Market Talk

1208 GMT - Rio Tinto's CEO says shareholders "shouldn't be too worried" about the escalating tariff war, saying the world's second largest miner by value will navigate any changes in trading between nations. Rio Tinto both runs big operations in and is a big supplier of foreign commodities into the U.S., Jakob Stausholm tells investors in London. "There might be certain things that go in our favor, there might be certain things that don't go in our favor," he says. Aluminum tariffs pose a challenge for the miner, which is the biggest aluminum producer in the West and ships significant amounts of the metal into the U.S. from Canada. In that business, "there will be an impact, but it is manageable," Stausholm says. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

Base Metals Lower as Economic Growth Risks Weigh — Market Talk

0933 GMT - Base metal prices trade lower after U.S. President Trump's sweeping tariff plan, with LME three-month copper down 1.9% to $9,513.5 a metric ton and three-month aluminum slipping 0.5% to $2,477.50 a ton. The U.S. will impose a minimum 10% levy on most imported goods and even higher rates on some nations. However, the reciprocal tariffs won't apply to imported steel and aluminum, which are already subject to a 25% tariff. Copper was also exempted from these new tariffs, but an investigation on copper imports is still underway and tariffs could be announced in a matter of weeks. "Metals tariffs were largely left unchanged [Wednesday]," analysts at Morgan Stanley say. However, "the [economic] growth implications have more potential impact on metals markets, worsening the outlook for industrial metals." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

U.S. Tariffs Pose Downside Risks to Oil Demand Growth Estimates — Market Talk

0856 GMT - Global oil-demand growth estimates for this year and next face downside risks after the U.S. unveiled its latest salvo of levies, according to DNB Markets. Oil, gas and other refined products are reportedly among the exempted goods, sparing energy markets from direct impact. However, harsher-than-expected tariffs on major U.S. trade partners pushed crude futures lower on concerns that a widening trade war could stifle global growth. "Given the obvious risk of a global trade war and lower global economic growth, it is fair to say the risk is skewed to the downside for our global oil demand estimate of 1 million barrels a day year-on-year for 2025 and 2026," says Helge Andre Martinsen, senior energy analyst at DNB. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

Gold Rally Could Be Curbed by Fewer Rate Cuts and Firmer Dollar This Year, HSBC Says — Market Talk

0846 GMT - Escalating trade risks and geopolitical uncertainty will continue to drive gold's rally in the near-to-medium term, but U.S. monetary policy and a firmer dollar could weigh on the safe-haven asset, HSBC says. "The imposition of tariffs and retaliatory tariffs continues to buoy gold," chief precious metals analyst James Steel says. Central-bank buying is also expected to remain strong this year and next, buoyed by geopolitical risks and dollar diversification. However, "not all may be smooth sailing for gold. Although a risk-off asset, holding gold is not without risk itself." Fewer interest-rates cuts than previously projected and a firmer U.S. dollar have the potential to cap gains. Easing geopolitical risks could also undermine prices, while institutional-investor demand could be countered by the negative impact of high prices on physical demand for jewelry and coin, Steel says. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

Iron Ore Drops After Trump's 'Liberation Day' Tariffs — Market Talk

0421 GMT - Iron ore prices are lower in early Asian trade after President Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs. Trump imposed a 34% reciprocal tariff on imports from China, on top of an earlier 20%. The tariffs are much higher than expected, and expected to weigh on iron ore prices in the short term, China Galaxy Futures says in a research note. Iron ore prices are volatile in the near term amid fluctuations in macro expectations, they say. The losses are partially offset by signs of stronger demand, with active spot buying at ports, as construction activity picks up, according to ANZ Research. The most-traded iron ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is down 0.4% at CNY788.00 a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

Australian Consumers May be Tariff Winners, Iron Ore Miners Potential Losers — Market Talk

0245 GMT - Australian consumers might benefit from President Trump's latest tariffs, should reduced-price surplus European and Asian goods be redirected away from the U.S. and into Australia, says Whitefield managing director Angus Gluskie. But this could be a negative for Australia's big iron ore producers, who ship vast amounts of the steel ingredient to mills in Asia, he says. Still, the Sydney-based investor reckons the greatest impact on Australia from the sweeping U.S. tariffs is likely to be from how it jolts the global economy. "Disruption and uncertainty are difficult for corporations and consumers," says Gluskie. Tariff-driven inflation in the U.S. may also drive up global interest rates, he says. "So concerns around potential global stagflation are likely to remain the larger concern for investment markets." (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

Australian Aluminum Industry Body Says Trade Flows at Risk — Market Talk

0144 GMT - Australia's aluminum industry is concerned that President Trump's trade agenda may distort international trade flows, reducing market efficiency, says Australian Aluminium Council CEO Marghanita Johnson. In a statement, Johnson says the industry body is working to understand the impact of latest tariffs on Australia's aluminum trade. Trump already introduced a 25% tariff on the industry, and said it would not be subject to the latest "reciprocal" tariffs. Johnson welcomed a sharpened focus on antidumping by Australia's government, and reiterated a call for the metal to be included on Australia's critical minerals list. Aluminum is Australia's top manufactured export. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)
